name: Rongo
description: Rongo, also known as Lono in some Polynesian traditions, is a prominent figure in Polynesian cosmology, particularly revered in the Cook Islands, Tahiti, and New Zealand (as Rongomātāne). He is primarily associated with agriculture, peace, and fertility. Rongo's influence extends to cultivated plants, particularly the kumara (sweet potato), a staple crop in many Polynesian societies. He is often depicted as a benevolent deity who provides sustenance and prosperity to his followers. The veneration of Rongo involves elaborate rituals and ceremonies aimed at ensuring bountiful harvests and maintaining harmony within the community. His role contrasts with that of other deities like Tūmatauenga (god of war) highlighting the multifaceted nature of the Polynesian pantheon. Understanding Rongo's significance provides valuable insight into the agricultural practices, social structures, and spiritual beliefs of ancient Polynesian civilizations. He represents the importance of sustainable resource management and the interconnectedness between humanity and the natural world.
subtitle: God of Agriculture and Peace

keyMyths.title: Rongo and the Origin of the Kumara
keyMyths.description: The myth recounts Rongo's journey to the heavens to retrieve the kumara, a sacred food that would sustain his people. He faced numerous challenges and obstacles, demonstrating his unwavering dedication to ensuring the well-being of humanity. This myth highlights the importance of agriculture and the divine origins of essential resources in Polynesian culture. The successful retrieval of the kumara established Rongo as a benefactor and provider.
keyMyths.source: Grey, Sir George. *Polynesian Mythology and Ancient Traditional History of the New Zealand Race*. John Murray, 1854.

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: In some traditions, Rongo played a crucial role in separating Rangi (sky father) and Papa (earth mother), who were locked in a tight embrace, preventing light and life from entering the world. Rongo, along with his siblings, struggled to push them apart, ultimately creating space for humanity to flourish. This act demonstrates Rongo's contribution to the creation of the world and the establishment of order.
keyMyths.source: Beckwith, Martha Warren. *Hawaiian Mythology*. University of Hawaii Press, 1970.

keyMyths.title: Rongo and the Cycle of Seasons
keyMyths.description: Rongo's influence is closely tied to the cycle of seasons, particularly the planting and harvesting seasons. Rituals and ceremonies dedicated to Rongo are performed to ensure favorable weather conditions and abundant harvests. These practices demonstrate the deep connection between the deity and the natural rhythms of the environment, reflecting the importance of agricultural sustainability in Polynesian society. The timing of these rituals is crucial for maintaining the balance between human activity and the forces of nature.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, Margaret. *The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend*. Canterbury University Press, 1995.

sources.author: Grey, Sir George
